FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ABCD 4.0 release, N=10,258 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional secondary data analysis of ABCD Year 2 data using modified Poisson regression to examine associations between cyberbullying and eating disorder symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Eating Disorders (2023); no indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The sole exposure variable is cyberbullying victimization/perpetration (assessed as online/text/social-media bullying). No phone/SM-specific estimate (time on SM, named platform, texting frequency, SMAQ, MPIQ, etc.) appears in any table, supplementary, or sensitivity analysis with its own coefficient paired with an individual-trait outcome. Step 2 not needed. Binding clarification: cyberbullying alone fails IC2. The cyberbullying measure is not a measure of SM use time or a qualifying SM/phone construct.

notes: Cyberbullying is the IV, eating disorder symptoms are the DV. Despite the cyberbullying measure referencing social media platforms by name (Instagram, Snapchat) in the question wording, the construct measured is victimization status (yes/no), not SM use. Under binding clarification 3, cyberbullying alone does not qualify for IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: The cohort is the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study - a Dutch cohort started in 2003-2004 in Amsterdam, with 645 Dutch adolescents aged ~15.8 at T1 (2019-2021). This is the Dutch ABCD cohort, explicitly listed as NOT_MET in the criteria ('Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD)'). The paper explicitly states: 'data were retrieved from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study (Van Eijsden et al., 2010).'

notes: This is the Dutch ABCD cohort (Amsterdam Born Children and their Development), not the U.S. NIH-funded ABCD Study. The shared acronym is coincidental. The cohort is Dutch, started 2003-2004, community sample from Amsterdam. FT-EC1 applies.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 4.0 release, baseline 2016-2018, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, ages 9-10 at baseline, N=8,085.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear regression and multiple imputation to examine associations between ACEs, bullying victimization, and internalizing/externalizing problems in ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Youth and Adolescence (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate (time on SM, named platform, texting, SMAQ, MPIQ, app use, etc.) appears in any table or supplementary analysis with its own coefficient. The bullying victimization variable is a composite of cyberbullying, overt bullying, relational bullying, and reputational bullying combined into a single dichotomous or cumulative score; no separate cyberbullying-alone coefficient is reported anywhere. Step 2 not needed. Binding clarification: cyberbullying alone fails IC2, and here cyberbullying is not even reported separately - it is one sub-component of a broader bullying composite. The main IVs are ACEs score and composite bullying victimization; neither qualifies as a phone/SM-specific variable.

notes: The study examines ACEs and bullying victimization (a composite including cyberbullying, overt, relational, and reputational bullying) as predictors of internalizing/externalizing problems. Cyberbullying is never reported as a standalone variable with its own coefficient. Even if it were, cyberbullying alone does not qualify under IC2 binding clarification 3. FT-EC2 applies.
